title: 'Kicking Off' a New Year
summary: A Words and Their Stories episode about idioms drawn from American football. The host Anna Matteo explains 'kickoff' (start of an event or project, e.g. 'kick off a new year with a resolution'), 'punt' (handing a task to someone else or delaying a decision), and 'to move the goalposts' (changing rules so a goal becomes harder to reach). Examples and definitions are given for each expression, plus the related expression 'to move the target'. The piece ends by contrasting moving the goalposts (a football-only image) with moving the target (used across many sports).
key_facts: Sport: American football — source of the idioms discussed
Note: what Americans call 'soccer' is called 'football' in the rest of the world
Idiom: 'kickoff' = the start of an event/project; also used as phrasal verb 'kick off'
Score value: a successful field goal in American football is worth three points
Idiom: 'to punt' = to delay a decision or pass a task to someone else; synonym 'to table it'
Idiom: 'to move the goalposts' = changing the rules so a goal becomes harder
Idiom: 'a moving target' = a goal that keeps changing
Name: host/writer is Anna Matteo for VOA Learning English
Definition: 'idiom' — an expression whose meaning is not the literal sum of its words
Definition: 'goalpost' — one of two upright poles forming a goal

title: Knee Jerk and Gut Reactions: When We Are Not in Control
summary: Anna Matteo explains the idioms 'knee-jerk' and 'gut reaction'. 'Knee-jerk' comes from the medical reflex — an involuntary forward kick when a tendon below the kneecap is struck — and as an adjective describes predictable, automatic, often poorly considered reactions, usually used negatively. A 'gut reaction' is instinctual; it is a feeling that something is not right, and is usually used about oneself rather than others. The host gives a personal example of a hike in California where her gut reaction told her to turn back, and she later learned a dangerous bear had been sighted on that trail.
key_facts: Idiom: 'knee-jerk' = involuntary, automatic reaction; usually used negatively
Source: term comes from the medical knee-jerk reflex (a light blow on the tendon below the kneecap)
Definition (Merriam-Webster): 'an involuntary forward kick produced by a light blow on the tendon below the kneecap'
Idiom: 'gut reaction' = an instinctual feeling, often a warning that something is not right
Usage rule: 'knee-jerk' tends to describe other people's behaviour; 'gut reaction' is mostly used about oneself
Place mentioned: a mountain in California where the host was hiking
Story detail: a dangerous bear was later sighted in the same area she had fled
Writer/host: Anna Matteo for VOA Learning English
Words 'involuntary' and 'automatic' are key to understanding 'knee-jerk'
Definition: 'instinct' = behaviour based on automatic actions

title: Sometimes We 'Spread Ourself Too Thin'
summary: A Words and Their Stories episode about the idiom 'to spread ourselves too thin', meaning to take on too much. Anna Matteo explains the linked feelings — being overwhelmed, overextended, overcommitted — and introduces the synonym 'to bite off more than we can chew'. She notes that working on too many projects at once leads to none being done well, and that multitasking can be stressful. The lesson ends with a short dialogue between two teachers about students who failed a deadline because they were also rehearsing in a play and holding part-time jobs.
key_facts: Idiom: 'spread ourselves too thin' = take on too much; pushed to the limit
Synonym: 'bite off more than we can chew' = attempt more than is possible
Concept: doing many projects simultaneously may mean none is done well
Related word: 'multitasking' — performing several tasks at one time
Related word: 'overcommitted' — obligated beyond ability to fulfil
Definition: 'overwhelmed' — overcome by force, number, thought, or feeling
Dialogue example: students failing class because of nightly play rehearsals and part-time jobs
Teacher's response in dialogue: extend the deadline and warn students about overcommitment
Writer/host: Anna Matteo for VOA Learning English
Related term: 'rehearsal' = a private practice session before a public performance

title: 'Kitchen-Table' Is a Type of Politics
summary: Anna Matteo explains four labels used to describe types of politics. 'Kitchen-table politics' (also called dinner-table politics or bread-and-butter issues) covers everyday concerns such as rising gas and food prices that affect a family's daily decisions. 'Green politics' is centred on environmental sustainability, recycling and threats to wildlife. 'Identity politics' is shaped by personal characteristics such as gender, age, religion, and social/economic position. 'Gotcha politics' centres on damaging opponents through public accusations of wrongdoing.
key_facts: Term: 'kitchen-table politics' = issues affecting individuals and families day to day
Synonyms: 'dinner-table politics' and 'bread-and-butter issues' mean the same thing
Two example issues: rising gas prices and rising food prices
Merriam-Webster definition of 'bread-and-butter': 'as basic as the earning of one's livelihood'
Cambridge Dictionary definition: a subject related to most people's needs and interests
Term: 'green politics' = political issues focused on the natural environment
Three green-politics examples: environmental sustainability, recycling, threats to wildlife
Term: 'identity politics' = political views formed by personal experience (gender, age, religion, social/economic position)
Term: 'gotcha politics' = damaging opponents through accusations of wrongdoing
Etymology: 'gotcha' is short for 'I got you' — meaning 'I caught you'

title: What Does It Take to Be a 'Power Couple'?
summary: This Words and Their Stories explains expressions for people who are very close: 'joined at the hip' (nearly always together), 'thick as thieves' (very close), 'two peas in a pod' (lots in common), and 'kindred spirits' (sharing opinions and interests). It then introduces 'power couple' — two romantically involved people who are both highly successful, and notes the pair are most powerful when in complementary industries (e.g. architect + interior designer; music producer + famous singer). The host adds the affectionate term 'better half'. A short dialogue between two coworkers uses all of the expressions.
key_facts: Idiom: 'joined at the hip' = nearly always together
Idiom: 'thick as thieves' = very close
Idiom: 'two peas in a pod' = have many things in common
Idiom: 'kindred spirits' = share opinions and interests
Term: 'power couple' = two romantically involved people who are both highly successful
Example pairing 1 mentioned: a well-known architect and a famous interior designer
Example pairing 2 mentioned: a music producer and a famous singer
Term: 'better half' = affectionate term for one's spouse/partner
Term: 'sidekick' = a person closely associated with another as a subordinate or partner
Writer/host: Anna Matteo for VOA Learning English

title: Don't Miss a Thing With 'Eagle Eyes'
summary: Anna Matteo explores idioms inspired by birds' vision. 'Bird's-eye view' is an overall view from above; she uses it to describe the big-picture questions to ask at the start of any project. Raptors (eagles, hawks, owls) have powerful binocular vision — both eyes can focus on a single object — and special bone structures around their eyes that protect from sunlight. Hence 'eagle eye' or 'hawk-eyed' describes a person who notices distant or fine details that others miss. The Oxford English Dictionary dates the earliest written use of 'eagle eye' to 1567 by Roman Catholic theologian Thomas Stapleton.
key_facts: Idiom: 'bird's-eye view' = overall view, big-picture view
Idiom: 'eagle eye' = ability to notice distant objects or fine details
Idiom: 'hawk-eyed' = observant; watches and notices everything
Term: birds with sharp vision (eagles, hawks, owls) are called 'raptors'
Source cited: The Raptor Center at the University of Minnesota's College of Veterinary Medicine
Biology fact: raptors have binocular vision (both eyes focus on a single object)
Biology fact: eagles and hawks have a bone structure around the eyes that protects from bright sunlight
Date of first written use of 'eagle eye': 1567
Author of first written use: Thomas Stapleton, a Roman Catholic theologian
Source for date: the Oxford English Dictionary website

title: The Importance of Being 'Dialed In'
summary: Anna Matteo explains expressions that come from older technologies. A 'dial' is used to tune in to a channel; so to be 'dialed in' or 'tuned in' to a project means to be focused and to clearly understand what is going on. 'Don't touch that dial' and 'stay tuned' both mean keep watching/listening. A 'switch' is on or off — so 'to flip a switch' means to change something suddenly and easily. A 'needle' on a measuring device shows a reading — so to 'move the needle' is to cause a noticeable change, usually for the better.
key_facts: Term: 'dial' = a control for adjusting settings on equipment (radio, TV)
Idiom: 'dialed in' / 'tuned in' = focused; you clearly understand what is going on
Idiom: 'don't touch that dial' / 'stay tuned' = keep watching or listening
Term: 'switch' = on/off device for an electrical circuit
Idiom: 'to flip a switch' = make something happen quickly/suddenly
Idiom: 'to move the needle' = cause a noticeable change, usually positive
Example given: a restaurant changing to all organic food slowly was 'not a matter of flipping a switch'
Definition: 'static' = noise produced in a receiver by atmospheric or electrical disturbances
Definition: 'digital' = using calculation directly with digits rather than mechanical measurement
Writer/host: Anna Matteo for VOA Learning English

title: How to 'Dish Up' Something Good
summary: Anna Matteo explores the multiple uses of the word 'dish'. As a noun it can mean the object you serve food on, a specially prepared food, or an attractive person (somewhat dated). A 'signature dish' is the food a person is known for making — the host's is sweet potato rosemary dinner rolls. As a verb, 'to dish' means to share private information or gossip; the phrasal verb 'dish up' means to serve food, while 'dish out' is used for jokes or insults. The piece ends with the saying 'they can dish it out, but they can't take it', used about people who tease others but cannot stand being teased.
key_facts: Noun 'dish': (1) the object food is served on, (2) a specially prepared food, (3) an attractive person (dated)
Term: 'signature dish' = food a person is known for making
Personal example: the host's signature dish is sweet potato rosemary dinner rolls
Verb 'to dish' = to share private/personal information or gossip
Phrasal verb: 'dish up' = put food on a dish to serve; also sharing personal details
Phrasal verb: 'dish out' = give out jokes, insults, or harsh criticism
Saying: 'They can dish it out, but they can't take it' — said of people who give insults but can't accept them
Definition: 'tease' = make fun of someone
Definition: 'harsh' = excessively critical or negative
Writer/host: Anna Matteo for VOA Learning English

title: American Places — Gettysburg, Pennsylvania
summary: A travel feature describing Gettysburg National Military Park in eastern Pennsylvania, site of the most famous battle of the American Civil War. The Union Army of the Potomac under General George Meade fought the Confederate Army of Northern Virginia under General Robert E. Lee from 1 to 3 July 1863; about 170,000 soldiers fought, and more than 50,000 ended up dead, wounded, or missing. The Gettysburg Museum holds over one million Civil War artifacts and the famous cyclorama painting depicting Pickett's Charge. President Abraham Lincoln visited the battlefield on 19 November 1863 to deliver the two-minute Gettysburg Address.
key_facts: Place: Gettysburg National Military Park, eastern Pennsylvania
Date of battle: 1 to 3 July 1863
Total soldiers who fought: about 170,000
Casualties: more than 50,000 dead, wounded, or missing
Union commander: General George Meade (Army of the Potomac)
Confederate commander: General Robert E. Lee (Army of Northern Virginia)
Museum size: more than 1 million Civil War artifacts
Famous artwork: the Gettysburg cyclorama painting, which depicts Pickett's Charge
Date of Lincoln's visit: 19 November 1863, to dedicate a Union burial ground
Length of Lincoln's 'Gettysburg Address': just two minutes

title: Nepal Increases Cost of Permits to Climb Everest
summary: Nepal has raised the cost of a spring-season permit to climb Mount Everest from $11,000 to $15,000, the first increase in about ten years. Autumn-season permits rise from $5,500 to $7,500. Tourism director Narayan Prasad Regmi says the change will help fund waste removal and rescue operations on the world's highest mountain. Nepal hosts eight of the world's 14 mountains over 8,000 metres, including Everest at 8,849 metres. More than 400 climbers bought permits last year, bringing in about $4 million. Some operators worry climbers will shift to the Tibetan (Chinese) side instead.
key_facts: New spring-season permit fee: $15,000 (up from $11,000) — about a one-third rise
New autumn-season permit fee: $7,500 (up from $5,500)
Previous price had been unchanged for about 10 years
Height of Mount Everest: 8,849 metres
Number of mountains over 8,000 m worldwide located in Nepal: 8 out of 14
Climbers who bought Everest permits last year: more than 400
Permit revenue last year: about $4 million
Name of Nepal's tourism director: Narayan Prasad Regmi
Mountain operator quoted: Mingma G Sherpa of Imagine Nepal
Mountain straddles the border between Nepal and China
Money used for: waste removal and search-and-rescue operations
New rule: climbers must carry GPS trackers and bags for human waste

title: Cambodia Warns of 'Aggressive' Monkeys around Angkor Wat
summary: Cambodian officials warn visitors to Angkor Wat to stay away from wild macaque monkeys, which have become aggressive after being fed by tourists — especially YouTube influencers who use the videos to grow their channels. The APSARA National Authority, which runs the temple complex, says the monkeys now steal food, bite visitors, and push stones down from centuries-old temples. Angkor Wat is a UNESCO World Heritage Site (1992) in north-western Cambodia and was the capital of the Khmer Empire about 900 years ago. More than one million foreign tourists visited last year. Officials are looking for an 'appropriate solution' and have announced a census of monkeys in public areas.
key_facts: Place: Angkor Wat, north-western Cambodia
Type of monkey: macaque
UNESCO World Heritage Site status year: 1992
Age of complex: building work began about 900 years ago
Historical role: capital of the Khmer Empire
Foreign visitors last year: more than one million
Operator: APSARA National Authority
Spokesperson quoted: Long Kosal of APSARA
Main cause of problem behaviour: feeding by visitors and YouTube influencers making videos
Damage caused: biting visitors, climbing temples and pushing stones, damaging info signs
Planned response: a census to identify and remove dangerous animals
Definition: 'census' = an official count of a population in a given area

title: Yellowstone: The World's First National Park
summary: A descriptive monologue about Yellowstone, the world's first national park (established 1872). It covers almost 9,000 square kilometres, mostly in Wyoming, and sits on top of an active super volcano called the Yellowstone Caldera; a lake of hot liquid rock about 6 kilometres below the surface drives more than 300 geysers and hot springs — more than anywhere else on Earth. The most famous geyser, Old Faithful, erupts about every 90 minutes. The park sits on a high plateau averaging 2,400 metres, with January temperatures between -18 °C and -7 °C. Yellowstone Canyon is about 365 m deep and the Lower Falls drops 93 m. About 4,500 wild bison live there — the largest such group in the world.
key_facts: Year established: 1872 (the world's first national park)
Area: almost 9,000 square kilometres
Main state: Wyoming, in the western United States
Volcanic feature: Yellowstone Caldera — an active super volcano
Depth of underground magma lake: about 6 kilometres
Number of geysers: more than 300
Most famous geyser: 'Old Faithful' — erupts about every 90 minutes
Average elevation: 2,400 metres above sea level
January temperatures: between -18 °C and -7 °C
Yellowstone Canyon depth: about 365 metres
Drop of the Lower Falls: 93 metres
Bison population (2024): about 4,500 — the largest group of wild bison in the world
Year of major forest fires: summer 1988

title: Monarch Butterfly Count Nears 30-Year Low
summary: A feature on the Xerces Society's annual count of western monarch butterflies overwintering in California and Arizona. The 2024 count was just 9,119 butterflies — the second-lowest in 28 years and a 96 % drop from 233,394 in 2023. Causes blamed include pesticides, habitat loss (especially of milkweed, on which caterpillars feed), and a 2024 heatwave (Palm Springs reportedly hit 51.1 °C on 5 July). Biologist Emma Pelton notes monarchs suffer above 37.7 °C and die above 42.2 °C. Eastern monarchs winter in Mexico. The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service announced in December 2024 a proposal to list monarchs as 'threatened'.
key_facts: Conservation group: The Xerces Society for Invertebrate Conservation, based in Oregon
2024 western monarch count: 9,119
2023 count: 233,394 — a drop of 96 % in one year
Highest count on record: 1.2 million in 1997 (when the survey started)
Record low: 1,901 monarchs in 2020
Survey duration: 28 years (since 1997)
Sample site: Santa Barbara colony fell from 33,200 (last winter) to 198 (this year)
Heat tolerance: monarchs suffer above 37.7 °C; die above 42.2 °C
Palm Springs record heat: 51.1 °C on 5 July 2024
Caterpillar food plant: milkweed
Biologist quoted: Emma Pelton of the Xerces Society
Wintering location for eastern monarchs: Mexico
Wintering location for western monarchs: California coast
Proposed federal status (Dec 2024): 'threatened'

title: Australian Navy Rescues Rower Crossing Pacific from California
summary: A 44-year-old Lithuanian rower, Aurimas Mockus, was rescued by an Australian warship on 3 March 2025 after being trapped in Tropical Cyclone Alfred about 740 km off Queensland. He had set out alone from San Diego in October aiming to row 12,000 km solo and non-stop to Brisbane. Eighty-km/h winds destroyed most of his enclosed boat after three days adrift in the Coral Sea east of Mackay. The piece also recounts other Pacific solo crossings: Briton Peter Bird (1983, first to cross, towed last 48 km), John Beeden (2015, first true unbroken crossing), and Australian Michelle Lee (2023, first woman).
key_facts: Rower's name: Aurimas Mockus, age 44, from Lithuania
Rescue date: 3 March 2025, by Royal Australian Navy
Starting point: San Diego, California, October 2024
Target endpoint: Brisbane, Australia
Total intended distance: about 12,000 km
Distance from mainland when rescued: 740 km off Queensland
Cause of rescue: Tropical Cyclone Alfred, with 80 km/h winds
Time stranded: 3 days in the Coral Sea east of Mackay
Rescue coordinator: Australia's Maritime Safety Authority
First person to row Pacific solo (towed last 48 km): Peter Bird, Britain, 1983
First true unbroken Pacific solo crossing: John Beeden, Britain, 2015
First woman to make the crossing: Australian Michelle Lee, 2023 (Ensenada → Port Douglas)
Youngest attempt: Tom Robinson, age 24 in 2022; rescued near Vanuatu 2023 after 265 days

title: Learn New Skills with Microcredentials
summary: A feature explaining microcredentials — short professional-certificate programmes that focus on a narrow set of learning outcomes and can be finished in a week to a month. UNESCO sets the definition. Popular fields are data analytics, IT support, and cybersecurity; major providers include Google, Salesforce, Meta, Intuit, and IBM. A Coursera report found that 51 % of U.S. colleges offering microcredentials also provide academic credit, and some include the certificates in standard degree programmes (e.g. Hawaii Pacific University's business programme). Coursera fees: $59/month or $399/year. Coursera supports several languages including English, German, Spanish, French, Russian, Portuguese, Japanese, Korean, Chinese, and Arabic, plus auto-translation in others.
key_facts: Definition source: UNESCO defines microcredential as a professional certification on a narrow set of learning outcomes
Typical duration: from one week to one month
Three most popular fields: data analytics, IT support, cybersecurity
Big providers: Google, Salesforce, Meta, Intuit, IBM
Coursera figure: 51 % of U.S. colleges offering microcredentials also give academic credit
Example university: Hawaii Pacific University includes certifications in its business programme
Search tool mentioned: Credential Finder
Country example: Ukraine's 'ReSkill UA' initiative for skilled-worker training
Coursera monthly fee: $59
Coursera annual fee: $399
Coursera native languages: English, German, Spanish, French, Russian, Brazilian Portuguese, Japanese, Korean, traditional & simplified Chinese, Arabic
Source of report: Coursera

title: Students with Disabilities Praise Assistive Technology Using AI
summary: AI-powered assistive technology is helping students with disabilities — including dyslexia, vision problems, and speech disorders — through text-to-speech tools, summarisers, and chatbots that explain difficult work. The Individuals with Disabilities Education Act requires U.S. schools to provide such technology. Examples include 14-year-old Ben Snyder from Larchmont, New York, who uses 'Question AI' to outline reports in 15 minutes (versus 90 minutes), and 14-year-old Makenzie Gilkison near Indianapolis, who has dyslexia and was named to the National Junior Honor Society after using an AI chatbot and word-prediction software. The U.S. National Science Foundation funds the National AI Institute for Exceptional Education at the University of Buffalo.
key_facts: Federal law cited: Individuals with Disabilities Education Act
Federal funder of AI research: U.S. National Science Foundation
Research institute: National AI Institute for Exceptional Education, University at Buffalo, New York
Director quoted: Venu Govindaraju, University at Buffalo
Educational therapist quoted: Alexis Reid, Boston area
Case 1: Ben Snyder, 14, Larchmont, New York — uses 'Question AI' app
Speed gain: outline finished in 15 minutes instead of 90 minutes
Case 2: Makenzie Gilkison, 14, near Indianapolis, Indiana — has dyslexia
Outcome for case 2: named to the National Junior Honor Society
Tools used by Gilkison: AI-powered chatbot, word-prediction program, text-to-speech reader
Definition: 'dyslexia' = a brain condition making reading, writing and spelling difficult
Definition: 'chatbot' = a computer program that produces human-like writing or speech
Speech-to-text weakness noted: AI is less accurate for children's voices and handwriting

title: College Degree Remains Big Prize for Many Student-athletes
summary: Since a 2021 U.S. Supreme Court ruling, college athletes can earn money from 'name, image, and likeness' (NIL) deals. But most student-athletes say a degree still matters more than NIL money. Jonny Bottorff transferred to Northern Arizona University on a football scholarship. Indiana University basketball player Anthony Leal stayed for a fifth year to finish an MBA at the Kelley School of Business. Athletes can now use a 'transfer portal' to move schools without sitting out a year. UNLV's Matthew Sluka left over a disputed NIL payment, while Ray Harrison moved to Grand Canyon University and won two conference championships.
key_facts: Acronym: NIL = Name, Image and Likeness
Year of Supreme Court NIL ruling: 2021
Athlete 1: Jonny Bottorff, transferred to Northern Arizona University on a football scholarship
Athlete 2: Anthony Leal, basketball, Indiana University — pursuing an MBA at the Kelley School of Business
MBA progress mentioned: about 70 % through the Kelley programme
College-eligibility rule: athletes can play four seasons in a five-year window
Athlete 3: Ray Harrison, basketball, Grand Canyon University (Phoenix, Arizona) — won two conference championships
Athlete 4: Matthew Sluka, football, UNLV — left team over disputed NIL payment
Aspirational athlete: Tavarius Covington from Chicago wants to be first in his family to attend college
Big-name pro examples cited: Jayden Daniels (Washington Commanders), Caitlin Clark (Indiana Fever)
Mechanism: the 'transfer portal' lets athletes switch schools and play immediately

title: How to Lower Your Risk of Dementia Starting in Middle Age
summary: A Nature Medicine study by Dr Josef Coresh (NYU Langone Health) and colleagues predicts that about 1 million Americans will develop dementia each year by 2060 — roughly twice earlier estimates. People aged 55+ face up to a 42 % lifetime chance of dementia. Only 4 % develop it between 55 and 75; the risk jumps to 20 % by age 85 and 42 % between 85 and 95. Average lifetime risk in the study: 35 % for men, 48 % for women. Black Americans had a slightly higher risk (44 %) than white (41 %). Genetic risk includes the APOE4 variant. Risk can be lowered by controlling blood pressure, weight and diabetes, staying socially and cognitively active, and using hearing aids.
key_facts: Lead researcher: Dr Josef Coresh, NYU Langone Health
Publication: Nature Medicine
Predicted annual U.S. dementia cases by 2060: about 1 million
Lifetime dementia risk after age 55: up to 42 %
Risk between ages 55 and 75: only 4 %
Risk by age 85: 20 %
Risk between ages 85 and 95: 42 %
Men's lifetime risk in study: 35 %
Women's lifetime risk in study: 48 %
Black Americans' risk: 44 %; white Americans': 41 %
Study cohort: ~15,000 older adults followed from 1987 to 2020
Genetic risk factor: APOE4 gene variant
Specialist quoted: Dr James Galvin, University of Miami
Most common form of dementia: Alzheimer's disease
Other form mentioned: vascular dementia
Key prevention advice: control blood pressure, avoid obesity, exercise, stay cognitively active, use hearing aids

title: Saying 'No' at Work Can Be Good for Your Health
summary: A feature on setting workplace boundaries. Justin Stewart, a 36-year-old news production assistant, had to be hospitalised for exhaustion and infection after juggling several jobs. Tips given: take control of your time (Bobby Dutton of GBM6 plans his hardest task every Monday at 2 pm); practise 'no' responses out loud; workplace engagement coach Cara Houser advises declining without lengthy excuses; Amber Krasinski of IvyHill Strategies uses 'not yet' to soften refusals; New York psychotherapist Israa Nasir keeps a 'No List' and moved her Gmail app to her phone's second screen to break weekend email-checking. Experts also suggest using an email signature to flag working hours and holidays.
key_facts: Key worker example: Justin Stewart, 36, news production assistant
Outcome: hospitalised for extreme tiredness and infection
Doctor's quote: 'you're too young to be this stressed — you're going to have to quit something'
Event-company founder cited: Bobby Dutton of GBM6
Bobby Dutton's habit: every Monday at 2 p.m. plan the task most likely to be delayed
Workplace engagement coach: Cara Houser
Marketing-firm founder: Amber Krasinski, IvyHill Strategies — uses phrase 'not yet'
Psychotherapist: Israa Nasir, New York
Nasir's app trick: move Gmail from iPhone homepage to second page
Idiom: 'hustle' = to work rapidly and tirelessly
Suggested tool: use an email signature to share working hours and upcoming vacation
Key recommendation: take time before answering new work requests

title: Experts Provide Tips for Avoiding Computer-linked Vision Trouble
summary: About 104 million working-age Americans spend more than seven hours a day in front of digital screens. Heavy screen use can cause dry/watery eyes, headaches, blurred vision, and accelerate myopia, especially in children. The American Optometric Association president Steven Reed compares the strained focus muscle to holding up a light weight for hours. Key advice: follow the '20-20-20 rule' — every 20 minutes, look at something 20 feet (6 m) away for 20 seconds; children should add 2 hours of outdoor play (the 20-20-20-2 rule). Normal blinking is 18-22 times/min but drops to 3-7 at a screen. Blue-light glasses are not very effective. Stop screens 1-2 hours before bed.
key_facts: Number of working-age Americans on screens 7+ hrs/day: 104 million
Source of estimate: American Optometric Association
President quoted: Steven Reed, American Optometric Association
Vision conditions linked: dry/watery eyes, blurred vision, headaches, myopia (nearsightedness)
Authoritative body on blue light: American Academy of Ophthalmology — no permanent damage proven
Normal blinking rate: 18-22 times per minute
Blinking rate at a screen: only 3-7 per minute (Cleveland Clinic)
20-20-20 rule: every 20 minutes look 20 feet (6 m) away for 20 seconds
20-20-20-2 rule for kids: add 2 hours of outdoor play per day
Recommended screen-free time before bed: 1-2 hours
Doctor quoted on kids: Ayesha Malik, Children's Hospital of Philadelphia
Ophthalmologist quoted: Raj Maturi, Midwest Eye Institute, Indianapolis
Effectiveness of blue-light glasses: not very effective per AAO

title: Rise in ADHD Cases Raises Questions
summary: More than 15 million U.S. adults — roughly 1 in 17 — have been diagnosed with attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder, according to a recent government study. Diagnoses rose during the pandemic. Allison Burk of Columbus, Ohio, was diagnosed at 42 after her teenage daughter's testing revealed similar symptoms. Ohio State University psychologist Justin Barterian says assessment requests have doubled in two years. Over 7 million U.S. children have been diagnosed. Diagnostic-criteria changes in 2013 expanded the definition. The American Professional Society of ADHD and Related Disorders is preparing new adult-diagnosis guidelines, led by Margaret Sibley of the University of Washington.
key_facts: Adult U.S. ADHD diagnoses: more than 15 million (about 1 in 17 adults)
Child U.S. ADHD diagnoses: more than 7 million
Share of adults diagnosed at age 18 or later: about half
Patient example: Allison Burk of Columbus, Ohio — diagnosed at 42
Psychologist quoted: Justin Barterian, Ohio State University
Reported change: ADHD-assessment requests have doubled in 2 years
Year diagnostic criteria broadened: 2013
CDC researcher quoted: Angelika Claussen
ADHD shortage trigger: COVID-19 pandemic (March 2020) caused medication shortages
Psychologist leading guidelines: Margaret Sibley, University of Washington
Guideline body: American Professional Society of ADHD and Related Disorders
Diagnostic note: no blood or brain test exists — diagnosis relies on history and assessments

title: Study: Electrical Medical Device Helps Improve Muscle Strength
summary: Researchers at the University of Pittsburgh, led by Marco Capogrosso, implanted spinal-cord electrodes in three adults with spinal muscular atrophy (SMA). After a few hours per week of low-level electrical stimulation for one month, all three gained muscle strength, walked further in six-minute tests, and stood and walked more easily. The implant was originally developed to treat paralysis; this is the first test on SMA, a genetic disease that destroys motor neurons. Participant Doug McCullough, 57, said any improvement in a progressive disease is 'surreal'. Improvements faded a few months after the study. Findings appeared in Nature Medicine.
key_facts: Disease name: spinal muscular atrophy (SMA)
Disease type: genetic — destroys motor neurons in the spinal cord
Most-affected muscles: legs, hips, shoulders; sometimes breathing and swallowing
Study participants: 3 adults with SMA
Lead researcher: Marco Capogrosso, University of Pittsburgh
Publication: Nature Medicine
Trial length: one month
Stimulation duration: a few hours of electrical stimulation per week
Patient quoted: Doug McCullough, age 57
Result: all participants increased six-minute walking distance by the study's end
Effect duration: improvements decreased a few months after the device was switched off
Outside expert: Susan Harkema, neuroscientist (previously University of Louisville)
Original use of the implant: treating paralysis from strokes and spinal-cord injuries

title: How Daylight Saving Time Affects Health
summary: Daylight Saving Time started in the U.S. on 9 March 2025 at 2 a.m. and ends on 2 November. Hawaii, most of Arizona, Puerto Rico, American Samoa, Guam, and the U.S. Virgin Islands stay on standard time. Studies link the spring change to more heart attacks (on the Monday after) and strokes (for two days), plus more deadly morning car crashes. About a third of U.S. adults already get less than the recommended seven hours of sleep. The body's circadian rhythm runs on a ~24-hour cycle and is reset by morning light; the hormone melatonin rises in the evening. Tips: move bedtime 15-20 minutes earlier for several nights, get morning sunshine, and consider ending the time change altogether (a position held by the American Medical Association and American Academy of Sleep Medicine).
key_facts: DST 2025 start: Sunday, 9 March 2025 at 2 a.m.
DST 2025 end: 2 November 2025
U.S. areas that don't observe DST: Hawaii, most of Arizona, Puerto Rico, American Samoa, Guam, U.S. Virgin Islands
Recommended nightly sleep: 7 hours
Proportion of U.S. adults below that: about one third
Health risk 1: increased heart attacks on the Monday after DST starts (per American Heart Association)
Health risk 2: increased strokes for two days after
Health risk 3: rise in deadly car crashes in the days after
Body clock name: circadian rhythm (about 24 hours)
Hormone: melatonin — rises in the evening, causing tiredness
Reset mechanism: morning sunlight
Pre-change tip: shift bedtime 15-20 minutes earlier for several nights
Medical bodies favouring permanent standard time: American Medical Association, American Academy of Sleep Medicine

title: Private Lander Carrying NASA Experiments Lands on the Moon
summary: Texas-based Firefly Aerospace's 'Blue Ghost' lander touched down on 2 March 2025 on Mare Crisium on the moon's near side, making Firefly the first private company to land a spacecraft on the moon without crashing. Only five countries have done so: Russia, the U.S., China, India, and Japan. Blue Ghost is two metres tall and 3.5 m wide, named after a rare U.S. firefly, launched from Florida in mid-January carrying 10 NASA experiments. NASA paid $101 million for the spacecraft and $44 million for the equipment under its Commercial Lunar Payload Services (CLPS) program. Another lander (Intuitive Machines' Athena) was set to land 6 March near the lunar south pole; Japan's ispace 'Resilience' would attempt in about three months.
key_facts: Landing date: 2 March 2025
Landing site: Mare Crisium, near side of the moon
Lander name and company: Blue Ghost by Firefly Aerospace, Texas
First quoted: chief engineer Will Coogan — 'We're on the moon'
Top NASA science officer cited: Nicky Fox
Five countries with successful moon landings: Russia, United States, China, India, Japan
Lander dimensions: 2 m tall, 3.5 m wide, four legs
Launch month: mid-January (from Florida)
Number of NASA experiments carried: 10
NASA payment for spacecraft: $101 million
NASA payment for equipment: $44 million
NASA program: Commercial Lunar Payload Services (CLPS) — third mission under it
Operational period: one lunar day = about 14 Earth days
Other 2025 lander attempting March 6: Athena by Intuitive Machines, Houston, near the lunar south pole
Japanese lander 'Resilience' by ispace — second moon-landing attempt

title: A Short History of Spacecraft Landings on the Moon
topic: science / space — history of moon landings
summary: An overview of moon-landing history. The Soviet Union's Luna 9 made the first soft lunar landing in 1966 and proved the surface could support a lander. The U.S. Surveyor 1 followed in May 1966, paving the way for Apollo. Apollo 11's Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin landed in 1969; the U.S. sent 12 astronauts via Apollo 11-17 (Apollo 17, 1972) and remains the only country to land humans. China landed its first rover Yutu in 2013, Yutu-2 on the far side in 2019, returned 1.7 kg of near-side samples in 2020 and far-side samples in 2024. Russia's Luna 25 crashed in 2023. India's Chandrayaan-3 succeeded in 2023 on its second attempt. Japan's SLIM landed upside down in January 2024.
key_facts: First soft moon landing: Soviet Union's Luna 9 in 1966
First U.S. soft moon landing: Surveyor 1 in May 1966
First humans on the moon: Apollo 11 astronauts Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin, 1969
Apollo missions sending astronauts: 6 missions, 12 astronauts (Apollo 11-17, ending Apollo 17 in 1972)
NASA's next crewed lunar program: Artemis — aims to return astronauts by end of 2026
China's first rover landing: Yutu, 2013 (third country to land)
China's far-side rover: Yutu-2, 2019
Chinese lunar samples returned in 2020: about 1.7 kg from the near side
Russia's recent failure: Luna 25, 2023 (crash near lunar south pole)
Last successful Russian/Soviet moon mission before that: Luna 24, 1976
India's success: Chandrayaan-3, 2023 (fourth country to complete a moon landing)
Japan's landing: SLIM, January 2024 (fifth country) — landed upside down

title: Study Shows How Earth's Orbit Affects Ice Ages
summary: A study published in Science on 27 February 2025 by a team led by Stephen Barker of Cardiff University finds that, absent human emissions, Earth's next ice age would likely begin in about 11,000 years and end ~66,000 years from now. The researchers analysed three orbital factors first theorised about a century ago by Milutin Milankovitch — eccentricity (shape of Earth's orbit around the sun), axial tilt, and axial wobble — over 900,000-1,000,000 years of climate records (deep-ocean temperatures, ice sheets). The cycles of warming and cooling repeat roughly every 100,000 years; the last ice age ended about 11,700 years ago. Co-author Lorraine Lisiecki (UC Santa Barbara) said climate change over tens of thousands of years is not random.
key_facts: Publication: Science, 27 February 2025
Lead author: Stephen Barker, Cardiff University
Co-author: Lorraine Lisiecki, UC Santa Barbara
Theory origin: Milutin Milankovitch, Serbian scientist, ~100 years ago
Three Earth motions analysed: orbital shape (eccentricity), axial tilt, axial wobble
Warming-and-cooling cycle length: about 100,000 years
Climate-record length analysed: 900,000-1,000,000 years
Predicted next glaciation start: about 11,000 years from now
Predicted next glaciation end: in 66,000 years
Last ice age ended: about 11,700 years ago
Definition: 'glaciation' = a period in which large areas of ice form and move
Definition: 'axis' = the imaginary line around which Earth turns
Key caveat: only valid if human emissions do not override natural cycles

title: Methods for Protecting Earth against an Asteroid Strike
summary: NASA estimated a 3.1 % chance that asteroid 2024 YR4 (40-90 m wide) would hit Earth on 22 December 2032 — the highest probability ever recorded for a large rock in modern times. ESA planetary-defence head Richard Moissl says even at 100 % probability the world is 'not defenceless'. Methods being studied: NASA's 2022 DART mission (which successfully changed Dimorphos's orbit by hitting it with a spacecraft); a gravity tractor (a spacecraft flying alongside); ion-stream deflection; painting one side of the asteroid white to use sunlight pressure; nuclear weapons (more useful for kilometre-wide asteroids like the dinosaur-killer); and laser-beam ablation. As a last resort, the impact zone of a city-killer can be evacuated.
key_facts: Asteroid name: 2024 YR4
Estimated diameter: 40-90 metres
Estimated impact probability: 3.1 % on 22 December 2032
European Space Agency expert quoted: Richard Moissl
Planetary Society chief scientist quoted: Bruce Betts
Only deflection test ever performed: NASA's DART (Double Asteroid Redirection Test), 2022
DART target: Dimorphos, 160 m wide — orbit successfully changed
Non-impact method 1: gravity-tractor spacecraft flying alongside an asteroid
Non-impact method 2: emit a continuous stream of atoms to push the asteroid
Non-impact method 3: paint one side white so reflected sunlight slowly changes course
Last-resort method: nuclear weapon (suitable only for kilometre-scale asteroids)
Likeliness asteroid will miss Earth: about 97 %
Preparation window: 7.5 years

title: New Discovery Could Explain What Gives Mars its Red Color
summary: An international team led by Brown University (U.S.) and the University of Bern (Switzerland) report in Nature Communications that the mineral ferrihydrite — an iron oxide that forms in water-rich environments — is likely the source of Mars's red colour, contrary to earlier guesses of hematite. The study combined data from NASA's Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter, ESA's Mars Express and Trace Gas Orbiter, and rovers, with lab-made synthetic Martian dust. The widespread ferrihydrite suggests Mars once held large amounts of liquid water and could have supported life. Final confirmation awaits samples being collected by NASA's Perseverance rover.
key_facts: Newly proposed mineral cause: ferrihydrite (an iron-oxide mineral)
Previously hypothesised mineral: hematite
Lead institutions: Brown University (USA) and University of Bern (Switzerland)
Publication: Nature Communications
Lead researcher quoted: Adomas Valantinas, Brown University
Co-author: John Mustard, Brown University
NASA spacecraft used: Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter
ESA spacecraft used: Mars Express and Trace Gas Orbiter
Required conditions for ferrihydrite: oxygen plus water reacting with iron
Implication: Mars once supported a large supply of liquid water
Confirmation source: future samples returned by NASA's Perseverance rover
Mars nickname: 'the Red Planet'

title: Japan's Toyota Builds a City-like Center to Test Robotics, AI
summary: Toyota has completed Phase 1 of its 'Woven City' research site near Susono, Japan — a 47,000 m² complex (eventually 294,000 m²) built on the grounds of a closed Toyota plant. Project lead Daisuke Toyoda calls it a 'Living Laboratory' for robotics, AI, autonomous vehicles, hydrogen energy, and the movement of people, goods, information, and energy. All buildings are connected by underground passageways for self-driving deliveries. When fully open, about 100 'weavers' (employees and partner-company workers) will live there. Toyota chooses hydrogen as Woven City's energy of choice and is also planning to compete with Google's Waymo on self-driving services. Toyota does not expect the project to be profitable for the first few years.
key_facts: Project name: Woven City
Company: Toyota (Japanese automaker)
Location: near Susono, southern Japan
Phase 1 area: about 47,000 m²
Full planned area: 294,000 m²
Built on the grounds of: a closed Toyota automobile factory
Building operations began: 2021
Executive lead quoted: Daisuke Toyoda
Initial resident count: about 100 people, called 'weavers'
Concept name used by Toyota: 'Living Laboratory'
Featured energy source: hydrogen
Demonstration shown to AP: Japanese coffee maker UCC serving drinks from a self-driving bus
Auto expert quoted: Keisuke Konishi, Quick Corporate Valuation Research Center
Competitive target mentioned: Google's Waymo
Underground feature: all buildings are connected by underground passageways

title: Scientists Link Gene to Human Speech
summary: Researchers led by Dr Robert Darnell of Rockefeller University identified a human-specific variant of the protein NOVA1 that may have helped early humans develop spoken language. Using CRISPR gene editing, the team replaced the mouse version of NOVA1 with the human one; baby mice with the variant cried differently when their mother approached, and adult male mice gave different mating calls. The findings appear in Nature Communications. A previous gene linked to language, FOXP2 (identified by British scientists in 2001), is also found in Neanderthals — but the NOVA1 variant exists only in modern humans. Liza Finestack of the University of Minnesota was an external commentator.
key_facts: Gene/protein highlighted: NOVA1
Lead researcher: Dr Robert Darnell, Rockefeller University, New York
Publication: Nature Communications
Editing technique used: CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing
Test subjects: laboratory mice
Earlier speech-linked gene: FOXP2 (discovered by British scientists in 2001)
Key contrast: NOVA1 variant is found only in modern humans; FOXP2 variant is shared with Neanderthals
Human relatives mentioned: Neanderthals and Denisovans
Outside expert: Liza Finestack, University of Minnesota (speech and hearing)
Observed effect 1: baby mice with the human variant made different sounds for their mother
Observed effect 2: adult male mice with the variant made different mating sounds
Implication: may eventually identify children needing speech support early in life
Definition: 'CRISPR' = a genome-editing tool that cuts and modifies DNA sequences

title: American Inventors: Rocket Scientist Robert Goddard
summary: NASA calls American physicist Robert Goddard 'the father of modern rocket propulsion'. More than 100 years ago he tested solid-fuel rockets, made and tested the first soft-chemical (liquid) fuel rocket engine in 1925, and successfully launched the world's first liquid-fuel rocket in 1926 — a flight many historians rank with the Wright brothers' first airplane. The Smithsonian Institution funded his research and published reports such as 'A Method of Reaching Extreme Altitudes', which described the maths of rocket flight and suggested a rocket could one day reach the moon — a claim widely ridiculed at the time.
key_facts: Subject: Robert Goddard, American physicist and rocket scientist
NASA's title for him: 'the father of modern rocket propulsion'
Year of first soft-chemical (liquid) fuel rocket engine: 1925
Year of first liquid-fuel rocket flight: 1926
Equivalent achievement: as important as the Wright brothers' first airplane flight
Early supporter and funder: the U.S. Smithsonian Institution
Famous publication title: 'A Method of Reaching Extreme Altitudes'
Goddard quote: 'The dream of yesterday is the hope of today and the reality of tomorrow.'
Bold prediction: a rocket might one day reach the moon — widely ridiculed at the time
Definition: 'propulsion' = a force that pushes something forward
Definition: 'mission' = a flight into space aimed at reaching a goal

title: Scientists Discover Stonehenge-like Circle in Denmark
summary: Danish archaeologists discovered 45 ancient wooden pieces arranged in a 30-metre-wide circle during construction work in the north-western town of Aars. The find, announced by Sidsel Wahlin of the Vesthimmerlands museum, suggests cultural links between Denmark and the builders of Britain's Stonehenge, whose first stones were placed around 3000 BC. Other wood circles had been found previously on the Danish island of Bornholm but never one this large that could be properly studied. Archaeologists also unearthed a leader's grave, an ancient settlement, and a bronze sword, and they are now searching for ritual deposits such as flint arrowheads.
key_facts: Place: Aars, north-western Denmark
Number of wooden pieces found: 45
Diameter of circle: about 30 metres
Researcher quoted: Sidsel Wahlin, Vesthimmerlands museum
Possible cultural link: builders of Britain's Stonehenge
Approximate date of first Stonehenge stones (British Museum): around 3000 BC
Earlier comparable site: wood circles on the Danish island of Bornholm (Baltic Sea)
Other finds at the site: a leader's grave, an ancient settlement, a bronze sword
Term searched for: 'ritual deposits' (e.g. flint arrowheads, small knives or daggers)
Definition: 'henge' = a kind of circular Bronze Age structure
Source agency: AFP, adapted by John Russell for VOA Learning English

title: Total Lunar Eclipse to Turn Moon Red
summary: A total lunar eclipse — also called a 'blood moon' for its reddish-orange colour — was set for the night of Thursday 13 March 2025 into Friday 14 March, visible across the Western Hemisphere. Total eclipse began at about 2:26 a.m. Eastern Daylight Time, with the moon fully shadowed near 3 a.m. EDT, and lasted about an hour. The red colour comes from sunlight passing through Earth's atmosphere. Lunar and solar eclipses happen 4-7 times per year; the previous total lunar eclipse was in 2022. Ancient Greek thinker Aristotle observed that Earth's curved shadow during a lunar eclipse proved the Earth is round. The next total lunar eclipses will be 7 September 2025 and March 2026.
key_facts: Eclipse date: Thursday 13 March into Friday 14 March 2025
Best-viewing regions: North America and South America
Eclipse start time: 2:26 a.m. Eastern Daylight Time
Fully-shadowed time: near 3 a.m. EDT
Total visibility duration: about one hour
Nickname: 'blood moon'
Reason for red colour: sunlight passing through Earth's atmosphere
Frequency of lunar/solar eclipses: 4-7 per year (NASA)
Previous total lunar eclipse: 2022
Next total lunar eclipses: 7 September 2025 and March 2026
Planetarium director quoted: Shannon Schmoll, Abrams Planetarium, Michigan State University
Yale astronomy expert quoted: Michael Faison
Historical fact: Aristotle used Earth's curved shadow as evidence that Earth is round

title: France Sets New Record in Hunt for Nuclear Fusion
summary: On 12 February 2025, French scientists at the CEA (France's Atomic Energy Commission) sustained hydrogen plasma for a record 1,337 seconds (about 22 minutes) — 25 % longer than the previous Chinese record set the month before. Fusion mimics the energy reaction at the Sun's centre, in which two atomic nuclei fuse into a heavier one, releasing huge amounts of clean energy. The team used a tokamak — a doughnut-shaped device that heats hydrogen to about 50 million °C and confines the plasma with strong magnets. CEA's Anne-Isabelle Etienvre says technological barriers remain before fusion produces more energy than it consumes. Multinational ITER (USA, China, EU, India, Japan, South Korea, Russia) in southern France is delayed to at least 2033.
key_facts: Date of record-setting experiment: 12 February 2025
Plasma duration achieved: 1,337 seconds (about 22 minutes)
Margin over previous record: 25 % longer than China's record set one month earlier
Organisation: France's Atomic Energy Commission (CEA)
Device used: a tokamak (circular ring confining plasma with magnets)
Hydrogen temperature inside tokamak: about 50 million °C
Target temperature for actual fusion energy production: more than 100 million °C
CEA researcher quoted: Anne-Isabelle Etienvre, head of fundamental research
Reaction principle: nuclei of two atoms fuse into a heavier atom, releasing large amounts of energy
Multinational project: ITER — the world's largest tokamak, in southern France
ITER partner countries: USA, China, EU, India, Japan, South Korea, Russia
ITER operations now delayed until: at least 2033

title: Pearl S. Buck: The First American Woman to Win a Nobel Prize in Literature
summary: American writer Pearl S. Buck lived nearly 40 years in China, where she grew up as the daughter of religious workers Caroline and Absalom Sydenstricker. Her novel The Good Earth (1931) — the story of Chinese farmers Wang Lung and O-Lan — became the year's top U.S. seller and won her the Pulitzer Prize for fiction in 1932. In 1938 she became the first American woman to win the Nobel Prize in Literature. She wrote more than 100 books, plus short stories, poetry, plays, essays and children's books. She studied philosophy at Randolph-Macon Woman's College in Lynchburg, Virginia. She married John Lossing Buck in China, later moved to Green Hills Farm in eastern Pennsylvania, and died in 1973 aged 80.
key_facts: Subject: Pearl S. Buck — American novelist
Year of birth-to-death: died 1973, aged 80
Best-known novel: The Good Earth (1931 U.S. top-seller)
Pulitzer Prize for fiction: 1932 (for The Good Earth)
Nobel Prize in Literature: 1938 — first American woman to win it
Total books: more than 100
Parents: Caroline and Absalom Sydenstricker (religious workers in China)
Time spent in China: nearly 40 years
Higher-education institution: Randolph-Macon Woman's College, Lynchburg, Virginia
Subject studied: philosophy
Husband: John Lossing Buck (a religious worker who studied agriculture)
Main characters in The Good Earth: Wang Lung and his wife O-Lan
Final home: Green Hills Farm, eastern Pennsylvania
Themes she wrote about later: war, politics, religion, equal rights, education
